statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
On a field of size $ n \times m $ , there is a robot located at point $ (0, 0) $ , whose task is to reach point $ (n, m) $ . The robot moves in steps: at each step, it can move one unit to the right or one unit up. In other words, if it is currently at point $ (i, j) $ , it can move to either $ (i + 1, j) $ or $ (i, j + 1) $ in one step.

You do not know the exact route of the robot, but you need to intercept it. For this purpose, you have a jammer with power $ r $ , which you can place at any integer point $ (x, y) $ on the field ( $ 0 \le x \le n $ ; $ 0 \le y \le m $ ).

The robot is considered to be intercepted if at some point during its movement it is within a distance of no more than $ r $ from the jammer. In other words, if there exists a point $ (i, j) $ on the robot's path such that $ \sqrt{(i - x)^2 + (j - y)^2} \le r $ .

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2199G/1bae2c2e168b5a0d87f057a0ade314d74a23f69a1d64f9cf593f28af2e2824fa.png)But there is a problem: if you place the jammer too close to the starting or the ending point of the robot's route, it will be noticed, and your plan will be compromised. Thus, you want to position the jammer so that it covers neither the point $ (0, 0) $ nor the point $ (n, m) $ (i. e., the distance to each of them must be strictly greater than $ r $ ).

Calculate the number of suitable points for placing the jammer. A point is suitable if it is not too close to the ends of the route, but at the same time, the robot will be intercepted regardless of the route it takes.

Input Format
The first line contains one integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 100 $ ) — the number of test cases. The following are the $ t $ test cases.

The first and only line of each test case contains three integers $ n $ , $ m $ , and $ r $ ( $ n, m \ge 1 $ ; $ n \cdot m \le 10^9 $ ; $ 1 \le r \le n + m $ ) — the dimensions of the field and the power of the jammer.

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the number of points suitable for placing the jammer.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
6
1 1 1
10 5 3
3 6 3
4 2 3
1000000000 1 2
1 1000000000 499999999
Output
0
16
6
0
1999999992
4

Note
One possible placement of the jammer for the second test case is illustrated in the statement.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2199G - Jammer
Without loss of generality, let's assume that $$$n \le m$$$. Otherwise, we can simply "reflect" the field and the problem will remain unchanged.
Let's pay attention to two "special" paths of the robot: on one path, the robot will first go up to the limit and then to the right, while on the other path, it will go to the right to the limit and then up. It is not difficult to prove that our jammer will definitely intercept the robot if and only if it intercepts it on both these special paths.
Let's understand where to place the jammer so that it intercepts the robot on the path $$$(0, 0) - (0, m) - (n, m)$$$. For this, we need to place the jammer at a position $$$(x, y)$$$ such that either $$$x \le r$$$ (the robot will be intercepted on the segment $$$(0, 0) - (0, m)$$$), or $$$y \ge m - r$$$ (the robot will be intercepted on the segment $$$(0, m) - (n, m)$$$). Similarly, for the path $$$(0, 0) - (n, 0) - (n, m)$$$, we will get either $$$y \le r$$$ or $$$x \ge n - r$$$.
Then, let's iterate the coordinate $$$x$$$ of the jammer from $$$0$$$ to $$$n$$$ and determine all suitable coordinates $$$y$$$. What restrictions does the first path impose? If $$$x \le r$$$, then all $$$y \ge \left\lfloor \sqrt{r^2 - x^2} \right\rfloor + 1$$$ are suitable for us (we simply cannot choose points that are too close to $$$(0, 0)$$$). Otherwise (if $$$x  \gt  r$$$), only $$$y \ge m - r$$$ is suitable for us. Thus, the first path imposes a lower bound on possible $$$y$$$-s.
Similarly, if $$$n - x \le r$$$, then all $$$y \le m - 1 - \left\lfloor \sqrt{r^2 - (n - x)^2} \right\rfloor$$$ are suitable for us (meaning they are sufficiently far from $$$(n, m)$$$). Otherwise (if $$$n - x  \gt  r$$$), only $$$y \le r$$$ is suitable for us. Thus, the second path imposes an upper bound on possible $$$y$$$-s. Therefore, suitable $$$y$$$-s form some segment, and their number depends only on the length of that segment.
As a result, for each $$$x$$$ in $$$[0, n]$$$, we can count all $$$y$$$ in constant time. Thus, the complexity of the solution is $$$O(t \cdot n)$$$ (or $$$O(t \min(n, m))$$$ in general). Since $$$n \cdot m \le 10^9$$$ and $$$n \le m$$$, it follows that $$$n \le \sqrt{10^9}$$$.
